coredata.dc:title: Ni-NCDs promoted photocatalytic benzylamine oxidation coupled hydrogen production in Cu-In-Zn-S/WO3·H2O Z-scheme heterojunction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xianjin
Chen, Qitao
Khan, Afaq Ullah
Li, Lixia
Deng, Bangya
Jiang, Tianyao
Liu, Yanhong
Liu, Naiyun
Mao, Baodong
coredata.dc:description: The construction of Z-scheme heterojunction is important for efficient photocatalytic organic oxidation coupled hydrogen production. However, current methods suffer from poor synergy of charge extraction and surface reactions. In this study, we combined Cu-In-Zn-S (CIZS) quantum dots with WO3·H2O (WO) nanosheets to form a Z-scheme heterojunction, which was further modified with bifunctional N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) to improve charge extraction and abundant Ni sites for hydrogen evolution. The unique 0D/2D/0D CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs heterojunction resulted in a hydrogen production rate of 4.261 mmol g−1 h−1, with ascorbic acid, 8.06 times higher than that of CIZS. In addition, the selectivity of N-benzylidenebenzylamine from benzylamine oxidation reaction has been increased from 31.65 % to 73.34 %. This improvement can be attributed to the efficient charge separation, transfer, and surface reactions accelerated by the multifunctional Ni-NCDs. A series of photoelectrochemical tests and radical scavenging experiments confirmed the Z-scheme photocatalytic process and the important roles of superoxide radicals and holes. Photo-induced electrocatalysis tests further confirmed the accelerated hydrogen evolution reaction in the presence of Ni-NCDs. Furthermore, time-resolved photoluminescence test demonstrated that the synergies of NCDs and Ni significantly extended the charge carrier lifetime. These findings provide valuable insights into efficient hydrogen production through the modulation of Z-scheme heterojunctions using bifunctional metal-carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ⅰ-Ⅲ-Ⅵ quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Z-Scheme heterojunction
Ni-carbon dots
Hydrogen production

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CIZS QDs, (b) Ni-NCDs, (c) WO and (d) CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. Insets in (a) and (b): size distribution of the CIZS QDs and Ni-NCDs. (e) HRTEM, (f) STEM and (g) corresponding elemental mapping images of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of the samples. XPS spectra of (c) S 2p for CIZS and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, (d) N 1 s and (e) Ni 2p for Ni-NCDs and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, and (f) W 4 f for WO and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CIZS, WO, Ni-NCDs, CIZS/WO, and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. (b) Tauc plots and (c) XPS valence band spectra of CIZS and WO. (d) Mott-Schottky plots of Ni-NCDs. (e) Corresponding band alignment diagram.

(a) Amount of the time-dependent hydrogen production amount and (b) hydrogen production rate with AA. (c) Wavelength dependence of AQE of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs nanocomposite in comparison with the absorption spectrum. (d) Cycle stability of hydrogen production over CIZS and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs.

(a) The H2 and NBBA formation rates and (b) selectivity of BA to NBBA over CIZS, CIZS/WO-5 %, and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs −7 % in 5 h. (c) The H2 and NBBA formation rates and (d) selectivity BA to NBBA over CIZS/WO-X% with different proportions of WO. (e) The H2 and NBBA formation rates and (f) selectivity of BA to NBBA over CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-X% with different proportion of Ni-NCDs.

(a) EIS, (b) transient photocurrent and (c) PL spectra of CIZS, CIZS/WO and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. (d) TRPL curves of CIZS, CIZS/WO, CIZS/WO/NCDs, CIZS/WO/Ni and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. (e) LSV curves of CIZS, WO, CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs and Ni-NCDs in 0.5 M PBS under light irradiation. (f) The varying effects of distinct free radicals scavengers on the photocatalytic degradation of RhB utilizing CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. (g-h) EPR spectra with DMPO of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs.
Proposed photocatalytic hydrogen production mechanism of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs.
The proposed reaction pathway of photocatalytic BA oxidation coupled with H2 production.
Schematic diagram for the synthesis of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite materials.

head.ce:title: Ni-NCDs promoted photocatalytic benzylamine oxidation coupled hydrogen production in Cu-In-Zn-S/WO3·H2O Z-scheme heterojunction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Energy Research, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of Z-scheme heterojunction is important for efficient photocatalytic organic oxidation coupled hydrogen production. However, current methods suffer from poor synergy of charge extraction and surface reactions. In this study, we combined Cu-In-Zn-S (CIZS) quantum dots with WO3·H2O (WO) nanosheets to form a Z-scheme heterojunction, which was further modified with bifunctional N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) to improve charge extraction and abundant Ni sites for hydrogen evolution. The unique 0D/2D/0D CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs heterojunction resulted in a hydrogen production rate of 4.261 mmol g−1 h−1, with ascorbic acid, 8.06 times higher than that of CIZS. In addition, the selectivity of N-benzylidenebenzylamine from benzylamine oxidation reaction has been increased from 31.65 % to 73.34 %. This improvement can be attributed to the efficient charge separation, transfer, and surface reactions accelerated by the multifunctional Ni-NCDs. A series of photoelectrochemical tests and radical scavenging experiments confirmed the Z-scheme photocatalytic process and the important roles of superoxide radicals and holes. Photo-induced electrocatalysis tests further confirmed the accelerated hydrogen evolution reaction in the presence of Ni-NCDs. Furthermore, time-resolved photoluminescence test demonstrated that the synergies of NCDs and Ni significantly extended the charge carrier lifetime. These findings provide valuable insights into efficient hydrogen production through the modulation of Z-scheme heterojunctions using bifunctional metal-carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Under the bifunctional Ni-NCDs modification, the Z-scheme heterojunction of CIZS/WO exhibits enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency through synergistic improvements in efficient charge transfer and surface reactions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Using solar energy for photocatalytic hydrogen production is considered the best way to address energy shortages and ecological damage [1–3]. However, the four-electron process of photocatalytic water splitting for oxygen evolution is extremely slow, severely limiting the production of hydrogen [4]. In view of this, adding hole sacrificial agents can significantly improve this problem. This process can be seen as converting the chemical energy contained in the sacrificial agent into hydrogen energy for storage. However, the energy stored in hydrogen is not enough to offset the consumption of sacrificial agents, and the oxidation products can also cause pollution to the environment [5]. To solve this problem, sacrificial agents are replaced with organic compounds such as methylfurans, methanol, benzyl alcohol and benzylamine (BA) to selectively oxidize into diesel fuel precursors, ethylene glycol, benzaldehyde, N-benzylenebutylamine (NBBA), and other value-added organic compounds [6–10]. NBBA is an important intermediate in the synthesis of quinoline and oxazine compounds, which are of great significance in the fields of fine chemicals and pharmaceuticals [11]. Therefore, it is particularly important to combine hydrogen with NBBA production in terms of energy and economic consideration.

I-III-VI quantum dots (QDs) have many unique properties, such as quantum confinement effect, large surface area and high extinction coefficient in the visible region, which are widely used as excellent materials for photocatalysis [12–15]. However, when used in photocatalytic organic oxidation coupled with hydrogen evolution reaction, their performance was not satisfactory, because of the higher requirements for redox potentials and multiple active sites of the two reactions in one system [16,17]. A large number of studies have shown that constructing Z-scheme heterojunctions is a convenient and simple strategy to improve the energy level structure [18–20]. On this basis, specific adsorption of the substrates on different sites is carried out to couple the redox reactions together, which is the key to the further development of Z-scheme heterojunctions in this field. For instance, Hyun et al. added the Pt and IrOx cocatalysts on g-C3N4/TiO2 to achieve the coupling of redox reactions, greatly improving the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity [21]. Wang et al. directly designed a closely contacted Z-scheme heterojunction for organic oxidation coupled hydrogen evolution reaction, achieving highly selective oxidation of BA to NBBA and efficient hydrogen production [22]. However, there is limited research on the synergistic effects of charge transfer and surface reactions in Z-scheme heterojunctions, which hinders their further development.

Due to the inherent structural diversity in carbon dots (CDs), they possess various optical and electrictrial properties, making them useful in photocatalysis with versatile roles [23–26]. For example, CDs can increase light absorption [27], facilitate charge transfer [28], act as electron acceptors [29], and mediate active sites [30]. Furthermore, the numerous functional groups present in CDs allow for the formation of many derivatives [31]. Among these, metal CDs offer great flexibility in surface design, which renders great of opportunity for adsorbing specific molecules and thus optimizes surface reactions [32]. For instance, Wu et al. engineered Cu-CDs specifically for the photocatalytic oxidation of diludine, resulting in a significant improvement of photocatalytic efficiency [33]. In another example, NiPc-CDs were utilized to extract holes for the oxidation coupled hydrogen evolution reaction of benzyl alcohol with greatly enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [6]. These methods used CDs as rapid charge transfer channel and combined with metals acting as efficient active sites, which synergistically promotes the charge extraction and surface reaction coupling.

To achieve efficient charge separation and utilization for the coupled reactions, here we developed a modified Z-scheme photocatalyst by using Ni-modified N-doped carbon dots (Ni-NCDs) with superior charge extraction and reaction properties to combine with the Cu-In-Zn-S/WO3·H2O (CIZS/WO) to form the ternary CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite. Compared to pure CIZS QDs, the CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite show a significantly higher hydrogen production rate of 4.261 mmol g−1 h−1 with L-ascorbic acid (AA) for hole scarification, which is 8.06 times to that of CIZS. More importantly, the photocatalytic system achieved a high H2 production rate of selective oxidation of BA to NBBA, where the selectivity was dramatically promoted from 31.65 % to 73.34 % upon Ni-NCDs modification. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) test confirms the characteristic Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism. Additionally, our experimental results on free radical scavenging indicate the dominant role of the superoxide radical (·O2 -) and hole in the photocatalytic process. Furthermore, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) test demonstrated that the introduction of Ni-NCDs significantly extended the charge carrier lifetime. The photoelectrochemical activity test also provided evidence for the promoted hydrogen evolution with the presence of Ni-NCDs with abundant active sites. This study offers valuable insights into enhancing organic synthesis coupled hydrogen production by manipulating the Z-scheme heterojunctions with bifunctional metal-CDs, achieving required functions of redox capability, charge separation and surface reaction.

All the materials were of analytical grade, purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation, Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and used without any further purification. These reagents include ethylenediamine, copper(II) acetate monohydrate (Cu(OAc)2·H2O), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(OAc)2·2 H2O), indium nitrate (In(NO3)3·4.5 H2O), sodium tungstate (Na2WO4·2 H2O), nickel sulfate (NiSO4·6 H2O), thioacetamide, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), L-cysteine, L-ascorbic acid (AA), benzylamine (BA), N-benzylidenebenzylamine (NBBA), naphthalene, hydrochloric acid, absolute ethanol, citric acid, polyvinylpyrrolidone, rhodamine B, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, benzoquinone, isopropanol and deuterated chloroform.

Based on previous works, CIZS and WO were synthesized with minor modification [34,35]. The detailed synthesis methods can be found in the supporting information. CIZS/WO composite material was obtained through a hydrothermal method. In brief, 332 μL of 30 mg/mL CIZS solution were added to a 20 mL reaction vial, followed by addition of different amounts of 15 mg/mL WO solution that had been uniformly dispersed through sonication. Finally, the mixture volume was adjusted to 15 mL using deionized water and transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was treated at 110 °C for 4 hours. After natural cooling, the obtained sample was labeled as CIZS/WO-0.5 %. Similarly, by varying the amount of WO during synthesis, a series of samples with different ratios were prepared and denoted as CIZS/WO-1 %, CIZS/WO-3 %, CIZS/WO-5 %, CIZS/WO-7 %, CIZS/WO-10 %, CIZS/WO-15 % and CIZS/WO-20 %.

Based on previous work and with appropriate modifications, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized [6]. The specific synthesis method can be found in the supporting information. For the synthesis of Ni-NCDs, 200 mg of NCDs were thoroughly mixed with 20 mg of NiSO4·6 H2O and then dialyzed again for 72 hours before freeze-drying to obtain the Ni-NCDs powder.

The CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite material was synthesized through a simple mechanical stirring process. In brief, a centain amount of 10 mg/mL Ni-NCDs solution were added to a 14.925 mL CIZS/WO composite material solution. The mixture volume was adjusted to 15 mL using deionized water, and the mixture was stirred for 2 hours to obtain CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-0.5 %. Likewise, by adjusting the quantities of CIZS/WO and Ni-NCDs during synthesis, a series of samples with varying ratios were prepared and named as CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-1 %, CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-3 %, CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-5 %, CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-7 % and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs-9 %. The detailed procedure is shown in Scheme 1.

The morphological analysis of CIZS and Ni-NCDs photocatalysts was first performed by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as displayed in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. Both CIZS and Ni-NCDs exhibit a spherical morphology with sizes of 3.0 ± 0.5 nm and 1.4 ± 0.4 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 1c, WO exhibits a characteristic 2D nanosheet structure. Fig. 1d presents the TEM image of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, demonstrating the successful integration of 0D CIZS and Ni-NCDs onto the 2D nanosheets of WO, resulting in a closely interconnected 0D/2D/0D heterojunction structure. Furthermore, the insight into lattice structure is provided by the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs (Fig. 1e). The lattice spacings of 0.24, 0.26 and 0.21 nm correspond to the (121) plane of CIZS [36], (200) plane of WO [37] and (101) plane of graphite [38], respectively. The HRTEM images of CIZS QDs and Ni-NCDs are also provided in Figs. S1a and S1b, respectively. The scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and elemental mapping images of each element in CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs are depicted in Figs. 1f and 1g, which show the uniform distribution of all elements, including Cu, In, Zn, S, W, O, N, and Ni, indicating the successful loading of CIZS and Ni-NCDs on WO. These components are effectively dispersed onto the WO nanosheets, signifying the successful loading and even dispersion of Ni-NCDs and CIZS QDs in the nanocomposites.

To gain further insights into the structure of CIZS, WO, Ni-NCDs, and the composite materials, we conducted X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy characterizations. Fig. 2a dipicts the XRD patterns of each material. In case of CIZS, three distinctive peaks are observed at 27.9°, 46.4°, and 55.2°, corresponding to the (112), (204), and (312) crystal planes of Cu0.412In0.412Zn0.175S (JCPDS No. 41–1371), respectively [39]. As expected, WO exhibites sharp diffraction peaks at 16.5° and 25.6°, attributed to the (020) and (111) crystal planes of WO3·H2O (JCPDS No.84–0886) [40]. Meanwhile, the Ni-NCDs exhibit a broad peak at 24.0°, associated with the graphitic carbon [41]. In the XRD patterns of CIZS/WO and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, no significant alteration in the diffraction peaks is observed compared to CIZS. This indicates that the crystal structure of CIZS remains unchanged upon the introduction of WO and Ni-NCDs, possibly owing to the low loading amounts. In Fig. 2b, the Raman spectra of various materials are presented. The Raman signal of Ni-NCDs exhibts two peaks near 1350 and 1620 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of carbon materials, respectively [42]. Notably, these signals are also evident in the Raman spectra of the CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite. In addition, the split peak observed in the D-band of the Ni-NCDs (Fig. S2) spectrum is likely to be a consequence of the introduction of Ni elements [41]. Additionally, the peak at 948.8 cm−1 in WO originates from WO, while the peaks at 697.1 and 809.0 cm−1 can be attributed to W–O–W, and that at 267.1 cm−1 arises from O–W–O [43]. Due to the influence of the O–W–O signal, the Raman peak at 317.3 cm−1 in CIZS/WO and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs is no longer sharp but broadens with a spanning of 70.2 cm−1. Furthermore, the Fourier-transformed infrared spectra (Fig. S3) reveal new absorption peaks around 1016, 1140 and 1200 cm−1 in the CIZS/WO and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composites, associated with the stretching vibration of the S–O bond [44], which indicates a close integration of the components.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was throughly utilized to determine the local electronic environments of elements in CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite material (Figs. 2c–f and S4–5). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S4) reveals that all the relevant elements existed in CIZS, WO, Ni-NCDs and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. Fig. S5a reveals the binding energies of 931.8 and 951.7 eV for Cu+ 2p3/2 and Cu+ 2p1/2, respectively [45]. In Fig. S5b, the binding energies of In 3d can be identified at 444.6 and 452.1 eV, corresponding to In3+ 3d5/2 and In3+ 3d3/2, respectively [6]. As shown in Fig. S5c, Zn 2p exhibits two shake up satellite peaks at 1021.6 and 1044.6 eV that could be assigned to Zn2+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively [46]. As depicted in Fig. 2c, one can observe the binding energies of S 2p, specifically at 161.3, 162.5 and 164.5 eV, which correspond to S2- 2p3/2, S2- 2p1/2 and S–O bond, respectively [36,47]. XPS spectra of N are displayed in Fig. 2d, in which the binding energies of N 1 s is located at 399.8 eV, owing to pyridinic N [41]. N doping can change the electron density distribution and density of states in NCDs, which is conducive to promote the electron/hole separation and promoting the participation of photo-excited electrons in hydrogen evolution reaction [48–50]. Regarding Ni 2p in Fig. 2e, the two peaks at 856.6 and 873.8 eV originate from Ni2+ 2p3/2 and Ni2+ 2p1/2, respectively [51]. Additionally, two satellite peaks are located at 861.9 and 879.4 eV. Fig. 2f showcases the binding energies of W 4 f with readings of 34.5, 35.9 and 38.0 eV linked to W5+, W6+ 4 f7/2 and W6+ 4 f5/2, respectively [40,52]. The O 1 s peak (Fig. S5d) is fitted with three components, located at 530.9 and 532.9 eV, which are attributed to the lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen, respectively [53]. In summary, in comparison to bare CIZS, WO, and Ni-NCDs, alterations in the electronic binding energies of each element in the CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite material have occurred, reaffirming the close contact conducive to the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers among CIZS, WO, and Ni-NCDs. Specifically, the binding energies of Cu, In, Zn, and S elements in CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs tend to shift to higher positions compared to CIZS, indicating an outward flow of electrons from CIZS in the composite. In contrast, the reduced binding energies of Ni and N in Ni-NCDs within the composite suggest an inward flow of electrons [54]. A bold inference can be made that the photogenerated electrons from CIZS accumulate on the bifunctional Ni-NCDs and participate in reactions, with NCDs serving as the charge storage mediate and Ni2+ as the active site, which can accelerate the overall charge transfer and utinzation.

To comprehend the light-harvesting capabilities and band structure of the composite system, we employed UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, XPS valence band spectroscopy, and Mott-Schottky plot measurement for the materials. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra ( Fig. 3a) reveal the visible light absorption of CIZS, WO, Ni-NCDs, CIZS/WO, and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, where the introduction of Ni-NCDs with broad-spectrum absorption effectively expands the light absorption range of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. In addition, the same conclusion can also be drawn from the UV vis absorption spectra (Fig. S6). Based on the Tauc equation [55], the bandgaps of CIZS and WO are calculated to be 2.18 and 2.15 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b). Significantly, the valence band positions of CIZS and WO are determined to be 0.83 and 2.75 eV, respectively, through XPS valence band spectra (Fig. 3c). According to the formula
E
CB
=
E
VB
−
E
g
, the conduction band positions of CIZS and WO are located at 0.60 and −1.35 V (vs. RHE), respectively [56]. Furthemore, the flat-band potential of Ni-NCDs is found to be −0.44 eV through Mott-Schottky tests (Fig. 3d), and applying the Mott-Schottky equation yields an approximate conduction band position of Ni-NCDs at −0.40 eV [57]. Based upon the aforementioned data, we have delineated the energy level positions of the CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite system, as displayed in Fig. 3e. It can be concluded that the Z-scheme heterojunction is formed between CIZS and WO. This phenomenon is expected to facilitate the space separation of electron-hole pairs and enhance subsequent reduction capability for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

To assess the photocatalytic performance of the composite materials, we first used AA as a sacrificial agent. All the samples were exposed to light with wavelengths ≥ 420 nm for 5 hours. The results, as depicted in Fig. 4a, clearly demonstrate that the hydrogen production amount of the samples increases nearly linearly with time. In Fig. 4b, the CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs exhibits the highest hydrogen production activity (4.261 mmol g−1 h−1), surpassing CIZS (0.528 mmol g−1 h−1) and CIZS/WO (2.405 mmol g−1 h−1) by factors of 8.06 and 1.77, respectively. This significant enhancement may be attributed to the effective improvement in the charge separation efficiency of the Z-scheme heterojunction formed by WO and CIZS, and the addition of Ni-NCDs effectively mediating the charge extraction on the Z-scheme heterojunction to the Ni site for hydrogen evolution reaction. It is worthy noting that the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs is 1.53 times higher than that of CIZS/WO/NCDs (2.788 mmol g−1 h−1), which may be due to the addition of Ni that adds a large number of hydrogen evolution active sites. Furthermore, we conducted an in-depth investigation into the influence of WO and Ni-NCDs on the photocatalytic activity of the composite material (Fig. S7). Fig. S7a and S7b reveal that the hydrogen production of CIZS/WO initially increases and then decreases as the proportion of WO increases, with the optimal ratio identified as CIZS/WO-1 %. This behavior is linked to the fact that an appropriate amount of WO significantly promotes charge separation, while an excessive amount of WO tends to introduce a more pronounced optical shielding effect, resulting in decreased performance. Additional results regarding Ni-NCDs (Fig. S7c and S7d) indicate that the inclusion of bifunctional Ni-NCDs provides good charge transfer media and reaction sites for composite materials which integrats rapid charge seperation with efficient surface reactions, leading to a significant enhancement in photocatalytic activity. However, with a further increase in Ni-NCDs, excessive aggregation of Ni-NCDs leads to a performance decline. In addition, we conducted the photocatalytic activity test of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs at different wavelengths and calculated the corresponding apparent quantum efficiency (AQE), comparing it with the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 4c). As demonstrated in Table S1, the AQE reached up to 11.21 % at 420 nm, 8.17 % at 450 nm, 6.41 % at 485 nm, 2.88 % at 520 nm, and 0.13 % at 595 nm. Overall, as the wavelength increases, the AQE gradually decreases and becomes negligible when the wavelength exceeds 595 nm, which is in agreement with the absorption spectrum of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs. Last, to further explore the stability of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, we carried out 4 cycles of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution in Fig. 4d, which indicates that both materials perform well with reasonable stability. We compared the XRD before and after photocatalytic hydrogen evolution in Fig. S8 and found no significant change in the pattern, further indicating that its good stability.

Imines, as a class of high-value chemical intermediates, have important applications in pharmaceuticals and fine chemicals. Green and efficient oxidation of BA to NBBA is of great interest to couple with production of hydrogen through sunlight. (First, we confirmed that our main product was NBBA and the by-product was dibenzylamine by 1HNMR (Fig S9)). The activities of hydrogen and NBBA production are shown in Fig. 5. It can be seen in Figs. 5a and 5b that the hydrogen and NBBA production rates of pure CIZS are very low, only 0.009 and 0.001 mmol g−1 h−1. This may be due to the high valence band position of CIZS, which lacks the photo-generated hole oxidation ability to oxidize BA. The hydrogen and NBBA production rates of the CIZS/WO composite have been significantly improved, reaching 0.222 and 0.071 mmol g−1 h−1, respectively, with the selectivity of NBBA being improved to 31.65 %. This is mainly due to the Z-scheme heterojunction composed of CIZS and WO, which emloys the valence band position of WO and greatly enhances the oxydation ability of the photo-generated holes. Finally, by introducing NCDs and Ni-NCDs into the Z-scheme heterojunction system, the yields of hydrogen and NBBA are significantly improved. Among them, the hydrogen yields of CIZS/WO/NCDs and CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs reach 0.279 and 0.608 mmol g−1 h−1, respectively. The yields of NBBA reach 0.113 and 0.446 mmol g−1 h−1, respectively, with selectivities of 40.41 % and 73.34 %. This significant improvement reflects the unique advantage of Ni-NCDs as a redox unit with good adsorption capability of H on Ni sites and π-π conjugation with BA [6]. Overall, due to the different substrates and corresponding active sites of AA and BA, there are some differences in the activity variation on the composition of catalysts. The specific results are shown in Fig. 5c-f. As the WO component increases, the rates of hydrogen and NBBA production all show a volcanic trend of first increasing and then decreasing. This behavior is related to the fact that an appropriate amount of WO not only forms a Z-Scheme heterojunction with CIZS with greatly enhanced catalytic oxidation ability, but also provides the crystalline water in WO to effectively enhance its adsorption capacity for BA through intermolecular hydrogen bonding [22]. However, excessive WO appears turbid in the solution, severely blocking the system's light absorption ability and leading to a decrease in activity. As the composition of Ni-NCDs increases, the rates of hydrogen and NBBA production along with the selectivity of NBBA significantly increase (Figs. 5e-5f). At the same time, there is also a volcanic trend of first increasing and then decreasing behavior with increasing Ni-NCDs. This can be attributed to the excellent adsorption of H by Ni sites and the synergistic conjugation effect of delocalized π electrons in NCDs and benzene ring in BA. However, excessive Ni-NCDs may seriously affect the transfer of photo-generated charges and the exposure of active sites.

To gain further insight into the photocatalytic mechanism, a series of photoelectrocatalytic and active species tests were conducted. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent (TPC) plots ( Fig. 6a and 6b) indicate that CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs exhibits the smallest EIS arc radius and the highest photocurrent response compared to CIZS and CIZS/WO, which suggest the fastest charge transfer and the generation of the highest number of charge carriers. [58,59]. The PL spectra (Fig. 6c) reveal that CIZS demonstrate a PL peak at 624 nm, which can be effectively quenched by the introduction of WO and Ni-NCDs, indicating the improved charge separation. Next, to explore the lifetime of photogenerated electrons in the compsites, the TRPL measurement was uesd. As demonstrated in Fig. 6d and Table S2, the lifetime of CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs (62.39 ns) and CIZS/WO (35.23 ns) are significantly longer than that of CIZS (15.61 ns), further illustrating that the introduction of Ni-NCDs enhances the charge separation process and prolongs the charge carriers lifetime. Last, comparing the lifetimes of CIZS/WO (35.23 ns) vs. CIZS/WO/NCDs (51.58 ns) and CIZS/WO/Ni (36.12 ns) vs. CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs (62.39 ns) reveals the advantages of Ni and NCDs in terms of bifuntional synergy. The electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance of CIZS, WO, CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, and Ni-NCDs was analyzed using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) (Fig. 6e), among which Ni-NCDs exhibites the best HER activity under illumination, indicating its primary role in providing active sites. Moreover, to verify the Z-scheme mechanism of charge transfer, different free radical scavengers were added during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, as shown in Fig. 6f. The ·O2 - emerges as the sigificant contributor to photocatalytic degradation process, followed by h+. In addition, the presence of ·O2 - and ·OH in the CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs ternary composite material after irradiation is also proved by EPR using DMPO as the tropping agent (Fig. 6g and Fig. 6h). Based on the aforementioned information, the heterojunction formed between CIZS and WO adheres to a Z-scheme configuration.

Based on the above studies, a possible photocatalytic mechanism is shown in
Figs. 7 and 8. First, after visible light irradiation, the photo-generated h+ in CIZS recombines with the e- in WO, retaining their respective strong redox abilities of the rest photo-generated e- and h+. Next, due to the charge storage effect of NCDs, the e- in CIZS rapidly transfers to NCDs and ultimately reacts with H+ to produce hydrogen at the Ni site (Fig. 7). On the other hand, due to the π-π conjugation interaction between NCDs and BA, adsorbed BA by NCDs is rapidly oxidized by h+ to form BA radical cations. Then, under the promotion of OH-, the BA radical cation transforms from the N−center to the C−center, forming BA radicals. At the same time, OH- is converted to ·OH. Next, BA radicals react with ·OH to generate the NBBA intermediates (Ph–CH=NH) and water. Finally, the condensation reaction between Ph–CH=NH and BA produces NBBA (Fig. 8) [7].

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized a ternary photocatalyst of 0D/2D/0D CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs, using bifunctional Ni-NCDs to modify the CIZS/WO Z-Scheme heterojuncion via a simple hydrothermal method. By improving the charge separation efficiency and expediting surface reaction kinetics, this composite material exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activiy. The hydrogen production rate was increased 8.06 times from the initial 0.528 mmol g−1 h−1 (CIZS) to 4.260 mmol g−1 h−1 (CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs) upon AA as the sarificial agent. In addition, the selectivity of BA oxidation to NBBA was increased from 31.65 % to 73.34 %. Our findings suggest that ·O2 - and h+ are cricitcal in the photocatalytic process, proving the Z-scheme photocatalytic process. EIS and TPC tests confirm that CIZS/WO/Ni-NCDs composite materials have excellent charge separation and transfer capabilities. Moverover, light-induced electrocatalytic tests demonstrate that Ni-NCDs have good HER activity, which indicates the versitile effect of NCDs for bridging Ni (HER sites) and accelerating charge transfer. This study provides valuable insights into efficient hydrogen production coupled with amine oxidation through the modulation of Z-scheme heterojunctions using bifunctional metal-CDs.

Baodong Mao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Naiyun Liu: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yanhong Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tianyao Jiang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Bangya Deng: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lixia Li: Resources, Investigation. Afaq Ullah Khan: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Qitao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Xianjin Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.
